description: The image displays the CrossMark logo (a red bookmark icon with adjacent 'CrossMark' text), which is a publisher's digital object identifier for content updates rather than direct scientific data visualization. Based on the provided contextual metadata, this Figure corresponds to mechanical property characterization data for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel samples subjected to annealing temperatures ranging from 700°C to 1200°C for 1 hour. The figure comprises engineering stress-strain curves demonstrating that the 700°C annealed sample exhibits distinct yield point phenomenon and brittle fracture characteristics with only 4.8% elongation, whereas the 900°C annealed sample achieves optimal tensile properties including ultimate tensile strength of 855 MPa, yield strength of 651 MPa, and 36.6% elongation, yielding the maximum strength-ductility product of 31 GPa%. Additional true stress-strain curves and work hardening rate plots reveal that samples annealed below 850°C display rapid initial work hardening followed by precipitous decline, while higher temperature treatments show sustained hardening capacity. The mechanical trends correlate with microstructural evolution including complete dissolution of κ-carbides above 900°C, progressive grain coarsening (α-grains growing from 3.5 μm to 68 μm and γ-grains from 5.0 μm to 36 μm), and gradual elimination of banded structures, establishing the critical relationship between annealing temperature, phase constitution, and mechanical performance in this low-density steel system.

This figure presents the temperature-time profile illustrating the thermomechanical processing and heat treatment schedule for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel. The left segment depicts the hot-rolling procedure: homogenization at 1200°C for 1 h, followed by multi-pass hot-rolling with a finishing temperature of approximately 880°C, subsequent water cooling to around 300°C, and final air cooling to room temperature. The right segment shows the annealing process, where the hot-rolled plates were reheated to various temperatures ranging from 700°C to 1200°C (with solid and dashed lines indicating different specific temperatures), held for 1 h, and subsequently water quenched. This schematic establishes the standardized processing route for preparing samples to investigate the effect of annealing temperature on microstructural evolution (including κ-carbide dissolution and phase fraction changes) and tensile properties, providing the thermal history foundation for correlating processing conditions with the resulting dual-phase (α+γ) microstructures and mechanical behavior observed in the study.

Figure 2 presents the equilibrium phase fraction diagram of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel calculated by Thermo-Calc software using the TCFE7 database. The graph plots temperature (°C) on the horizontal axis ranging from 600 to 1500 °C against phase fraction (0–1.0) on the vertical axis. Four distinct curves represent the equilibrium mass fractions of ferrite (α, BCC), austenite (γ, FCC), κ-carbide (κ), and liquid phase (L) as functions of temperature. The diagram reveals that ferrite (α) initially decreases with increasing temperature, reaching its minimum fraction around 1000 °C before increasing again. Austenite (γ) dominates the intermediate temperature range (800–1300 °C) with a maximum fraction of approximately 0.7. The κ-carbide appears below the eutectoid temperature of 823 °C via the reaction γ → α + κ, as indicated by the arrow. Liquid phase (L) formation begins above approximately 1350 °C. This thermodynamic calculation predicts that δ-ferrite forms first during solidification, followed by peritectic austenite formation, and provides the theoretical baseline for understanding κ-carbide dissolution behavior during annealing treatments.

Figure 3 presents the microstructure of the hot-rolled (HR) Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel, comprising optical microscopy (OM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images. Panel (a) shows an OM micrograph of the RD–ND plane, revealing a typical banded structure with alternating coarse α-ferrite (Al-rich, indicated by red arrows) and γ-austenite (Mn and C-rich, indicated by blue arrows) phases elongated along the rolling direction. Panel (b) displays an OM micrograph of the RD–TD plane, illustrating the inhomogeneous microstructure characterized by large continuous α-phase areas and γ-phase areas with non-uniform distribution. Panel (c) provides a high-resolution SEM micrograph of the RD–TD plane, clearly resolving κ-carbide (Mn and Al-rich, indicated by orange arrows) precipitates distributed along both α/γ phase boundaries and α/α grain boundaries. The scale bars indicate 50 μm for OM images and 2 μm for the SEM image. This figure establishes the baseline as-received microstructure, demonstrating the presence of banded ferrite-austenite duplex structure and intergranular κ-carbide precipitation prior to annealing treatments, which serves as the reference state for investigating microstructural evolution during subsequent annealing.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel comparing the hot-rolled (HR) condition with specimens annealed at temperatures ranging from 700°C to 1200°C for 1 hour. The stacked diffractograms display intensity versus 2θ (30–90°), with each curve offset vertically for clarity. Key phases identified include α-ferrite (BCC), γ-austenite (FCC), and κ-carbide (perovskite structure). At 800°C, distinct peaks for all three phases are visible, while at 700°C, κ-carbide peaks are present but γ-phase peaks are absent, indicating a nearly complete γ→α+κ eutectoid transformation. The HR specimen shows only α and γ peaks without detectable κ-carbide reflections, attributed to low volume fraction or coherency with the α-matrix. As annealing temperature increases to 900°C and above, κ-carbide peaks disappear, indicating dissolution into the matrix, leaving only α+γ dual-phase structures at higher temperatures. The caption and text report calculated lattice parameters: α-phase (2.89–2.91 Å), γ-phase (3.65–3.67 Å), and κ-carbide (3.76–3.79 Å), noting lattice expansion due to Mn, Al, and C solid solution. This figure provides critical evidence for the phase evolution sequence and κ-carbide dissolution temperature range (850–900°C), supporting the correlation between annealing temperature, microstructural constituents, and subsequent mechanical property variations.

This figure presents six optical microscopy (OM) micrographs illustrating the microstructural evolution of Fe-10Mn-10Al-0.7C low-density steel annealed at 800 °C, 850 °C, and 900 °C for 1 hour. Subfigures (a), (c), and (e) display the RD–ND (rolling direction–normal direction) sections, while (b), (d), and (f) show the RD–TD (rolling direction–transverse direction) sections. The images reveal three distinct phases: α-ferrite (marked by red arrows), γ-austenite (blue arrows), and κ-carbide (orange arrows labeled 'K'). At 800 °C ((a) and (b)), a pronounced banded structure persists with coarse α grains, and κ-carbide is visible mainly along α/γ and α/α boundaries. At 850 °C ((c) and (d)), the κ-carbide fraction decreases significantly, becoming primarily distributed along α/α boundaries while disappearing from the γ-phase areas, accompanied by an increase in γ-phase volume fraction. At 900 °C ((e) and (f)), the κ-carbide has almost completely dissolved into the matrix (with only trace boundary precipitation detectable), resulting in a dual-phase α+γ microstructure with reduced banding continuity. The scale bar indicates 50 μm for all images. These micrographs demonstrate that the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide lies between 850 °C and 900 °C, higher than the equilibrium calculation of 823 °C, and support the correlation between annealing temperature, phase volume fractions (α decreasing from ~62% to ~40%, γ increasing from ~29% to ~60%), and the gradual elimination of the banded morphology.

This figure presents a series of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs illustrating the microstructural evolution of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at temperatures ranging from 700 °C to 1200 °C for 1 hour. Subfigures (a) through (g) display the RD–TD (rolling direction–transverse direction) sections, while (h) shows the RD–ND (rolling direction–normal direction) section. At 700 °C (a), the γ phase has almost completely decomposed into lamellar κ-carbide and α matrix via eutectoid reaction, with continuous κ-carbide along grain boundaries. At 800 °C (b) and 850 °C (c), the κ-carbide coarsens into globular shapes and decreases in fraction, while γ phase fraction increases to approximately 29% and 39%, respectively. From 900 °C (d) to 1100 °C (g), κ-carbide dissolves completely (with only trace boundary precipitation during quenching), resulting in α+γ dual-phase microstructures with progressively increasing grain sizes. At 1200 °C (h), significant grain coarsening occurs (α grains reaching 68 μm), with needle-shaped FCC particles appearing within large α grains. The images demonstrate the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide lies between 850 °C and 900 °C, and reveal the transition from brittle eutectoid microstructure to ductile dual-phase structure as annealing temperature increases.

This figure presents TEM micrographs of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 700°C, revealing the microstructural evolution following eutectoid decomposition of γ phase into lamellar κ-carbide and α matrix. Panel (a) shows a bright-field (BF) image displaying alternating lamellar plates of κ-carbide and α-ferrite with clearly labeled phases and a 500 nm scale bar. Panel (b) provides the selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) viewed along [110]κ, with schematic indexing indicating a Nishiyama–Wasserman (N–W) orientation relationship between κ-carbide and α matrix: (110)α∥(1-11)κ and [001]α∥[011]κ. Panel (c) presents a (200)κ dark-field (DF) image taken from the [100]κ direction, highlighting the distribution of κ-carbide lamellae corresponding to panel (a). Panel (d) shows a BF image of globular or short-rod like κ-carbides dispersed in the α matrix, which exhibit no orientation relationship with the matrix, indicating loss of coherency during carbide coarsening. The figure demonstrates that at 700°C, while fine lamellar κ-carbides maintain crystallographic orientation with α, incoherent globular morphologies coexist, supporting the mechanism of carbide coarsening and its subsequent impact on tensile ductility.

Figure 8 presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of the Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimen annealed at 800 °C for 1 hour followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field (BF) micrograph revealing the coexistence of three phases: α-ferrite (BCC), γ-austenite (FCC), and κ-carbide (perovskite structure), with the κ-phase appearing as elongated rod-like or globular precipitates distributed along phase boundaries. The scale bar indicates 500 nm. Subfigure (b) displays selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) confirming the crystal structures and zone axes: α along [111], κ along [001], and γ along [011]. As reported in the text, examination of multiple areas revealed no orientation relationship between the coarsened κ-carbides and the neighboring α-matrix at this annealing temperature, indicating the loss of coherency that existed at lower temperatures (e.g., Nishiyama–Wasserman relationship at 700 °C). This microstructural evolution demonstrates that 800 °C annealing causes κ-carbide spheroidization and dissolution of the interfacial coherency, contributing to the observed mechanical property changes.

This figure presents a quantitative plot illustrating the evolution of average grain size as a function of annealing temperature (ranging from 900 °C to 1200 °C) in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel. The data, obtained from SEM micrographs, displays three distinct curves with error bars representing standard deviations: (1) α₂ phase (banded shape, pink diamonds), representing ferrite that solidified first during casting and distributed continuously with large grains; (2) γ phase (blue triangles), indicating austenite; and (3) α₁ phase (red squares), representing ferrite mainly distributed among γ grains with relatively small grain sizes. The plot reveals that the α₂ ferrite exhibits the largest grain size (increasing from ~26 μm at 900 °C to ~68 μm at 1200 °C) and fastest growth rate with increasing temperature. The γ phase shows moderate grain growth from ~5.5 μm to ~36 μm over the same temperature range. Notably, the α₁ phase displays an initial slight decrease in grain size from 900 °C to 950 °C (from ~3.2 μm to ~2.5 μm) before increasing to ~22 μm at 1200 °C. These grain size variations are identified as the primary factor governing the tensile properties of the steel above 900 °C, following the Hall–Petch relationship, where larger grain sizes correspond to decreased yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and elongation. The figure distinguishes between the two ferrite morphologies (banded α₂ versus intergranular α₁) to explain the anomalous grain growth behavior compared to conventional Fe–Mn–Al–C steels.

This figure presents four scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the evolution of boundary κ-carbide in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 900 °C (a), 1000 °C (b), 1100 °C (c), and 1200 °C (d) for 1 hour. Subfigures (a) through (c) reveal continuous or semi-continuous κ-carbide precipitates (labeled "boundary κ") decorating the α-ferrite grain boundaries, with scale bars of 10 μm, 10 μm, and 5 μm respectively. Subfigure (d), at 15 μm scale, distinctly shows boundary κ-carbide alongside needle-shaped or plate-shaped FCC particles precipitated within large α grains. The micrographs demonstrate that boundary κ-carbide persists along α/α boundaries across the entire 900–1200 °C annealing range, forming during quenching when Mn, Al, and C segregate to grain boundaries. At 1200 °C, the intragranular FCC particles compete with boundary κ for Mn and C elements. These images support the conclusion that while κ-carbide dissolves in the matrix below 900 °C, boundary κ remains stable at higher temperatures, critically influencing the microstructural stability and mechanical property evolution of the steel.

This figure presents TEM characterization of boundary κ-carbide in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 1100°C for 1 hour. Panel (a) is a bright-field micrograph showing a κ-carbide precipitate (labeled κ, indicated by yellow arrow) located along the grain boundary between α-ferrite matrix grains (labeled α), with a scale bar of 500 nm. The accompanying panel displays selected area electron diffraction patterns confirming the crystal structures: the left pattern from α phase with zone axis z = [001] indexed with (-110) and (110) reflections, and the right pattern from κ-carbide with zone axis z = [1-12] showing (200), (110), and (-111) reflections. These images provide direct evidence that κ-carbide persists along α/α boundaries at high annealing temperatures (900–1200°C) despite dissolution in the matrix. The caption states the actual dissolution temperature is between 850°C and 900°C, higher than the 823°C equilibrium prediction by Thermo-Calc, attributed to calculation errors and disequilibrium effects during quenching.

This figure presents comprehensive TEM characterization of boundary κ-carbide in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel subjected to annealing at 1200 °C for 1 hour followed by water quenching. Panel (a) is a bright-field (BF) micrograph showing elongated κ-carbide precipitates (indicated by arrow and labeled κ) distributed along the grain boundary between α ferrite matrix regions (labeled α), with a scale bar of 200 nm. Panel (b) shows the selected-area diffraction pattern (SADP) obtained from both phases, revealing the Nishiyama-Wassermann (N-W) orientation relationship between the perovskite-structured κ-carbide and the body-centered cubic α matrix: specifically (110)α is parallel to (11-1)κ and [001]α is parallel to [011]κ. Panel (c) is a dark-field (DF) micrograph formed using the (200)κ reflection taken along the [110]κ zone axis, where only discrete segments of the boundary κ-carbide appear bright against the dark background, demonstrating that neighboring κ-carbide particles possess large orientation misalignment despite each maintaining the N-W relationship with the adjacent α matrix. These results confirm that boundary κ-carbide forms during quenching when the annealing temperature exceeds the κ-carbide dissolution temperature (between 850 °C and 900 °C), and the partial illumination in the DF image indicates that the carbide particles nucleate with different variants or undergo independent growth, resulting in high-angle boundaries between adjacent κ particles.

This figure presents SEM micrographs paired with EDS line scanning analysis of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at (a) 800 °C and (b) 900 °C for 1 hour. The upper panels show backscattered electron images revealing the microstructural evolution: at 800 °C, distinct κ-carbide particles (indicated by yellow arrows) are visible along α/γ and α/α boundaries within the ferrite (α) and austenite (γ) matrix, while at 900 °C, κ-carbides have largely dissolved leaving a dual-phase α+γ structure. The lower panels display quantitative EDS line scans (Al K, Mn K, Fe K) acquired along the indicated white lines, demonstrating elemental partitioning among phases. At 800 °C, pronounced composition peaks reveal that κ-carbide exhibits the highest Mn and Al contents compared to the surrounding α and γ phases, with Mn enrichment and Al depletion in γ relative to α. At 900 °C, the line profiles show uniform elemental distribution without κ-carbide signature peaks, confirming the dissolution of κ-phase. These results support the conclusion that κ-carbide serves as a strong sink for Mn and Al, that γ-phase is Mn-rich and Al-lean compared to α-phase, and that the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide lies between 850 °C and 900 °C, with higher thermal stability at α/α boundaries than at α/γ boundaries.

This figure presents the phase volume fraction evolution as a function of annealing temperature for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel, determined from XRD measurements using the direct comparison method. The plot displays three distinct curves: α-ferrite (red squares), γ-austenite (blue triangles), and κ-carbide (orange stars). The α phase peaks at approximately 0.84 volume fraction at 700°C, then decreases sharply with increasing temperature, stabilizing around 0.37–0.43 between 900°C and 1200°C. Conversely, the γ phase is absent at 700°C but increases rapidly with temperature, reaching approximately 0.60 at 900°C and peaking at 0.63 around 1000°C, before slightly declining to 0.57 at 1200°C. The κ-carbide shows a continuous dissolution trend, starting from approximately 0.15–0.16 in the hot-rolled (HR) state and decreasing to zero by 900°C, consistent with the caption note that κ-carbide cannot be detected by XRD at 900°C. These results indicate that the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide lies between 850°C and 900°C, which is higher than the equilibrium value of 823°C calculated by Thermo-Calc. The figure demonstrates that annealing between 900°C and 1200°C produces a stable dual-phase α+γ microstructure with phase fractions varying only within a narrow range (γ: 57%–63%, α: 37%–43%), providing essential quantitative data for correlating microstructural evolution with tensile properties in this low-density steel.

This figure presents the tensile mechanical properties of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at temperatures ranging from 700 °C to 1200 °C for 1 h. Panel (a) displays engineering stress–engineering strain curves, showing that the 700 °C specimen exhibits a distinct yield drop (discontinuous yielding) while all other specimens display continuous yielding behavior. The flow curves demonstrate a general trend of decreasing strength with increasing annealing temperature, with the 700 °C specimen reaching approximately 971 MPa ultimate tensile strength but only 4.8% elongation, whereas the 900 °C specimen achieves the optimal balance of 855 MPa strength and 36.6% elongation. Panel (b) presents true stress–true strain curves alongside work hardening rate curves, revealing that specimens annealed at 700–850 °C (containing κ-carbide precipitates) exhibit high initial work hardening rates that drop rapidly before failure, while specimens annealed at ≥900 °C (with dissolved κ-carbide) show lower initial work hardening rates but more sustained hardening behavior. The data support the conclusion that the dissolution of κ-carbide in the 850–900 °C range is critical for optimizing the strength-ductility product (reaching 31 GPa% at 900 °C), as the presence of continuous κ-carbide along grain boundaries at lower temperatures promotes cleavage fracture, while excessive grain growth at higher temperatures (>950 °C) reduces both strength and ductility.

This figure presents the tensile property evolution of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel as a function of annealing temperature (700–1200°C) with a fixed holding time of 1 hour, including the hot-rolled (HR) condition for comparison. The multi-axis plot displays four distinct curves: tensile strength (red squares, left y-axis, MPa), yield strength (magenta squares, left y-axis, MPa), ultimate elongation (green circles, right y-axis, %), and the product of strength and ductility (blue stars, right y-axis, GPa%). The graph reveals that both tensile and yield strengths decrease monotonically with increasing annealing temperature, dropping from approximately 1100 MPa and 1000 MPa in the HR condition to about 610 MPa and 495 MPa at 1200°C, respectively. In contrast, the ultimate elongation exhibits a non-monotonic trend, initially increasing from 4.8% at 700°C to a peak of 36.6% at 900°C, then decreasing to 4.8% at 1200°C. Consequently, the product of strength and ductility follows a similar trend to elongation, reaching its maximum value of approximately 31 GPa% at 900°C. These observations indicate that annealing at 900°C provides the optimal balance of strength and ductility, which caption states corresponds to the dissolution of κ-carbide and the establishment of a fine dual-phase microstructure consisting of approximately 40% α-ferrite and 60% γ-austenite.

This figure presents SEM fractographs of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at 800 °C, 900 °C, and 1000 °C for 1 hour. Low-magnification images (a, c, e) with 100 μm scale bars reveal well-developed secondary cracks running parallel to the rolling direction, which caption states are associated with the banded microstructure. High-magnification images (b, d, f) with scale bars of 10 μm or 25 μm display mixed fracture modes comprising ductile fracture (dimpled rupture attributed to γ-austenite) and quasi-cleavage fracture (river patterns attributed to α-ferrite). At 800 °C, the fracture surface shows a mixture of ductile and quasi-cleavage features with κ-carbides promoting cleavage along grain boundaries. The specimen annealed at 900 °C exhibits more pronounced ductile fracture regions compared to 1000 °C, correlating with optimal tensile ductility (36.6% elongation). At 1000 °C, the ductile fracture area becomes less apparent as grain coarsening occurs, indicating that the fracture mode evolution directly reflects microstructural changes in phase fractions and grain sizes with annealing temperature.
